body:
This is a regression from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90507.

Note that the existing check in place was also flawed, as it did not check whether `--test` was specified before `--`. If it's specified after `--`, Godot should ignore it.
author: Calinou

body: @Calinou Made a PR for this.
author: Dowsley

body: @akien-mga Maybe we can close this after merge of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/96926
author: Dowsley

body: Fixed by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/96926.
author: Calinou

body:
When using `@export`, `outro_node` references the SpriteSpawner node.

```ini
[node name="SpriteSpawnerHolder" parent="." node_paths=PackedStringArray("outro_node") instance=ExtResource("1_1sija")]
outro_node = NodePath("SpriteSpawner")
```

But with `@export_storage`, it's creating its own node.

```ini
[node name="SpriteSpawnerHolder" parent="." instance=ExtResource("1_1sija")]
outro_node = Object(Node2D,"_import_path":NodePath(""),"unique_name_in_owner":false,"process_mode":0,"process_priority":0,"process_physics_priority":0,"process_thread_group":0,"physics_interpolation_mode":0,"auto_translate_mode":0,"editor_description":"","visible":true,"modulate":Color(1, 1, 1, 1),"self_modulate":Color(1, 1, 1, 1),"show_behind_parent":false,"top_level":false,"clip_children":0,"light_mask":1,"visibility_layer":1,"z_index":0,"z_as_relative":true,"y_sort_enabled":false,"texture_filter":0,"texture_repeat":0,"material":null,"use_parent_material":false,"position":Vector2(0, 0),"rotation":0.0,"scale":Vector2(1, 1),"skew":0.0,"script":Resource("res://addons/meuplugin/SpriteSpawner.gd"))
```

So `add_child()` is working, the issue is `outro_node` not referencing the correct node.
author: timothyqiu

body:
> When using `@export`, `outro_node` references the SpriteSpawner node.
> 
> ```ini
> [node name="SpriteSpawnerHolder" parent="." node_paths=PackedStringArray("outro_node") instance=ExtResource("1_1sija")]
> outro_node = NodePath("SpriteSpawner")
> ```
> 
> But with `@export_storage`, it's creating its own node.
> 
> ```ini
> [node name="SpriteSpawnerHolder" parent="." instance=ExtResource("1_1sija")]
> outro_node = Object(Node2D,"_import_path":NodePath(""),"unique_name_in_owner":false,"process_mode":0,"process_priority":0,"process_physics_priority":0,"process_thread_group":0,"physics_interpolation_mode":0,"auto_translate_mode":0,"editor_description":"","visible":true,"modulate":Color(1, 1, 1, 1),"self_modulate":Color(1, 1, 1, 1),"show_behind_parent":false,"top_level":false,"clip_children":0,"light_mask":1,"visibility_layer":1,"z_index":0,"z_as_relative":true,"y_sort_enabled":false,"texture_filter":0,"texture_repeat":0,"material":null,"use_parent_material":false,"position":Vector2(0, 0),"rotation":0.0,"scale":Vector2(1, 1),"skew":0.0,"script":Resource("res://addons/meuplugin/SpriteSpawner.gd"))
> ```
> 
> So `add_child()` is working, the issue is `outro_node` not referencing the correct node.

I mean, that is an interesting observation, because it explains why ```add_child``` doesn't work, but still, I believe that there is something wrong with ```@export_storage```. Because, it was suppose to create a reference to the "SpriteSpawner" node right? Just like ```@export``` did.
author: GustavoLR548

body: I think this is likely caused by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/95520, it seemed this GPU got added to our blocklist

author: Calinou

body:
I assume you didn't compile your binary with ANGLE included? That might solve it.

Our logic to force ANGLE on old GPUs should probably be improved to still try native OpenGL if ANGLE isn't included. CC @bruvzg
author: akien-mga

body:
> I assume you didn't compile your binary with ANGLE included? That might solve it.

Indeed, I was compiling it on Linux with MinGW and didn't have ANGLE set up.

We should look into tweaking the error message, so that it doesn't imply that ANGLE was attempted when it wasn't compiled in. After the fix, Godot should be able to start again on this device, but you can still see the error message if trying to run Godot in an environment without any 3D acceleration (e.g. a VM).

Maybe we can also print a warning on startup when the device *should* be using ANGLE due to the blocklist, but it's not due to ANGLE not being compiled in.
author: Calinou

body: Maybe we should also have a dedicated `angle=yes/no` flag, currently if `angle_libs` is not set it's still building with ANGLE support but attempts to load `libEGL`/`libGLESv2` as dynamic libraries.

body:
It looks like the C# code is unable to find the method `Viewport.set_world_3d`. According to the `custom.py` you provided, you are building with 3D disabled, which means this method won't be registered in ClassDB.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/568589c9d8c763bfb3a4348174d53b42d7c59f21/scene/main/viewport.cpp#L4776-L4777

When using a custom build of the engine, make sure that the C# bindings you use match the same build configuration. Otherwise the C# bindings may contain methods that are not available in your custom build or other discrepancies.

To do this build the editor with the same build configuration you use for the template and then use it to generate the C# glue with `--generate-mono-glue`. Then, build the C# assemblies with `build_assemblies.py` and use the built packages in your C# project. See the [Compiling with .NET](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/contributing/development/compiling/compiling_with_dotnet.html) documentation for a more detailed explanation of these steps.
author: raulsntos

body:
@raulsntos Does this mean building the editor with `disable_3d=yes` as well? As far as I understand this isn't allowed.
How can it skip generating the bindings for 3D if the editor requires 3D to be enabled?

body:
That's expected behavior, you'd either need to to turn off physics interpolation specifically for that AnimatedSprite2D:
![Godot_v4 3-stable_win64_aRBRtIxh2t](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b2e83eb9-f21b-4545-8069-264e80bd001e)
Or reset in manually in code after the "teleportation":
```gdscript
func _test()->void:
	if Input.is_action_just_pressed(&"ui_accept") && !animated_sprite_2d.visible:
		if _vel.x > 0:
			animated_sprite_2d.position.x = 30
		elif _vel.x < 0:
			animated_sprite_2d.position.x = -30

		animated_sprite_2d.show()
		animated_sprite_2d.reset_physics_interpolation() # <-- Currently must be after `show()` see comments below.
		animated_sprite_2d.play()
		await animated_sprite_2d.animation_finished
		animated_sprite_2d.hide()
```

Otherwise it interpolates with whatever its previous position was, meaning it possibly interpolates between `(30, 0)` and `(-30, 0)`, right after you're showing it.

Better visible with lower `physics/common/physics_ticks_per_second` project setting:
![zdDY1bzRwa](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b05301b6-f219-40fd-93af-f37f999e7c8d)

---

Seems like documentation issue, should be solved by https://github.com/godotengine/godot-docs/pull/9738.

body:
> but, animated_sprite_2d.reset_physics_interpolation(),the problem still exists

Oh, indeed. Currently `animated_sprite_2d.reset_physics_interpolation()` would need to be after `animated_sprite_2d.show()` to take effect, see: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/94736#issuecomment-2251369114. I've edited my comment.

(I've tested it before and it worked, so I must have moved the `animated_sprite_2d.reset_physics_interpolation()` line closer to the "teleporation" code after pasting it in here.)

---

@lawnjelly @rburing Shouldn't `is_visible_in_tree()` be changed to `is_inside_tree()` in this check? :thinking:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/568589c9d8c763bfb3a4348174d53b42d7c59f21/scene/main/canvas_item.cpp#L377-L381
author: kleonc

body:
It generates a line mesh for basic shape debug purposes so it has no faces like a full triangle mesh. You can not directly use a line mesh array to make a triangle mesh with the surface tool as they use different array layouts which is why your surface tool example mesh misses triangles.

A shape does not really hold mesh information like a rendering mesh does. It only has basic convex polygon data everything else is discarded on import. So while going from rendering mesh to collision shape is somehow common and supported going the other way is rare. You can still do it with custom scripts and creating procedural meshes but there is no functionality that does it with a single function call or button press.

Line meshes are preferred for shape debug as they are more performant to render and create less visual clutter, e.g. you usually do not want a character be fully covered by a collision debug that has rendered faces or has artifacts from transparency all over the place.
author: smix8

body:
I see!
I was intent on using get_debug_mesh for my use case but what I really should have been looking to use is get_faces() for ConcavePolygonShape3D, and rasterizing every other kind of shape into a mesh on a case-by-case basis, since they aren't treated like a standard trimesh by physics or debug to begin with. I had a misunderstanding of how Shape3D worked. Thank you for the information, happy to close this.

body:
Thanks for the report! Consolidating in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/76436.

LOD in MeshInstance3D adjusts the LOD of all instances at once. To my knowledge, this is based on the distance between the camera and the MultiMeshInstance3D node's origin. I'm not sure if the AABB's size is taken into account (if it was, it would probably take the size of one instance into account, not the entire MultiMeshInstance3D's AABB).

author: darksylinc

body:
I cannot reproduce it on my two machines:

 - AMD Radeon 6800 XT (AMD CPU)
 - AMD Radeon RX 560 (Intel i7 7700 CPU)
 - Intel HD 630 (Intel i7 7700 CPU)
 - NVIDIA 1060 3GB (Intel i7 7700 CPU)

Both machines use Ubuntu 20.04.04 LTS.

This could still be an NV issue that only manifests on newer GPUs.

This _may_ be a Vulkan Loader issue (basically the system that loads the actual Vulkan driver; and on Linux it is provided by the distro).

Personally I've tested loader versions 1.2.131.2-1 (the one provided by Ubuntu 20.04) and loader 1.3.261.1 (one I installed by hand).

You can test the Loader theory by installing one by hand:

1. Download Vulkan SDK from its homepage https://vulkan.lunarg.com/sdk/home#linux
2. Extract it somwhere, e.g. /home/username/vulkan
3. Launch Godot with the following environment variables:

```bash
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/home/username/vulkan/1.3.261.1/x86_64/lib
export PATH=/home/username/.local/share/mold/bin:/home/username/.local/bin:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin:/usr/games:/usr/local/games:/snap/bin:/home/username/.dotnet/tools:/home/username/vulkan/1.3.261.1/x86_64/bin
export VK_LAYER_PATH=/home/username/vulkan/1.3.261.1/x86_64/etc/vulkan/explicit_layer.d
export VULKAN_SDK=/home/username/vulkan/1.3.261.1/x86_64
./godot.linuxbsd.editor.x86_64 -e
```

But I've got a better understanding now: The Editor build sets `DEBUG_ENABLED` flag, which includes the extra code that is causing the crash.

I'm not sure if the code from #90993 intended for it to be enabled in the Editor.

A simple hotfix right now since users are crashing is to find the following in rendering_context_driver_vulkan.h:

```cpp
#if defined(DEBUG_ENABLED) || defined(DEV_ENABLED)
#define VK_TRACK_DRIVER_MEMORY
#define VK_TRACK_DEVICE_MEMORY
#endif
```

and change it to:

```cpp
#if defined(DEV_ENABLED)
#define VK_TRACK_DRIVER_MEMORY
#define VK_TRACK_DEVICE_MEMORY
#endif
```

Then rebuild. That _should_ fix the crash.

Though I'd want to chat with @Calinou to see if we can debug the issue on his machine since he can reproduce it.


**Update:** May I ask the output when launched with `--verbose` ?

body:
Coming back now to add: I just tested by putting 2 animationplayers (1 for each sprite) and separating the animations, animation player tracks, and animation trees, and all works fine. The source of the problem was that both animation trees were dependent on the same animationplayer.

Still, this is an inconvenience from 4.2.2 which allowed multiple trees to be attached to the same player. Maybe it'd be worth looking into, since at least for me, letting multiple trees be rooted to one player saves a lot of hassle and memory as compared to having to make a separate player for every little sprite and doubling the amount of nodes for animations.
author: adrianznur

body:
You must send MRP as it is not clear what you mean.

BTW, if you mean a case where two separate AnimationTrees play different animations based on the same animation player, but with different trackpaths for each, I think if you disable the Deterministic option and set the AnimationTree's CallbackModeDiscrete to Dominant or Recessive, the two AnimationTrees would work.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/44ed4796-bcb7-4e05-809a-8c3b3fe3ad8c)

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8fbbd620-547f-47ab-b9ee-128bd04fed77)

See also https://godotengine.org/article/migrating-animations-from-godot-4-0-to-4-3/#callbackmodediscrete.

If you mean any other case, we cannot tell without MRP. In any case, if multiple AnimationMixers animate the same property on the same object, there should be no bug there (as just seeing the issue title), since it is the intended behavior that only the last AnimationMixer is processed.

body:
Line 162
`float foam_mix = clamp(pow((1.0 - (depth + VERTEX.z) + foam_noise), 8.0), 0.0, 1.0);`
becomes
`float foam_mix = clamp(pow((0.25 / (depth + VERTEX.z) + foam_noise), 8.0), 0.0, 1.0);`

0.25 is now a variable that controls the amount of foam. I have now migrated to 4.3 and shader is different, this one change is only to make it work in 4.2. As seen on the top screenshot, for some reason on macOS it also takes into account distant depth as well as shallow depth to calculate the amount of foam.
author: aristides86

body:
This happens because `TileSetAtlasSource.use_texture_padding` is enabled for both sources, by default.
![3iXmivTBDo](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9166fa0f-4cdc-4c5a-a7ec-4adc3e5f200f)


Thus both sources generate new runtime texture with additional padding around tiles. Turning it off makes both sources use the original atlas (and draw with a single draw call because of no texture changes).

![Godot_v4 3-stable_win64_VilaZRjBmv](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1fd10c31-2fa2-4782-b1a3-b9278c18ef72)|![Godot_v4 3-stable_win64_DV0W9rjZCt](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2a934cdb-e6e8-4b97-ac9e-835699b85a05)
-|-


I'd say this is not a bug, but it could possibly be improved by detecting such situations and generating a shared padded texture for different sources. :thinking:

---



> ## godot 4
> 
> separate textures, 16x16 rect, 2 tile-types, same atlas texture = **34 drawcalls**

You've drawn 17x17 cells, just saying. And each draw call is per ~half row of tiles, as then there's a texture change.

body:
I'm pretty sure this is intended behavior, and inevitable, if your `WeaponNode` isn't a tool script.

Non-tool scripts don't get attached to nodes and resources during editor-time. They only get created during runtime.

author: maxime4000

body:
To reiterate: Godot does not, and should not, instantiate your custom classes, when creating nodes and resources, as long as those custom classes aren't marked at tools (using the "Tool" attribute). You may still be able to access these classes, but the generated nodes will only include anything that is permittet to be instantiated by the editor.

The information about what script is attached to a node/resource can be easily found using the "script" property. If you want your custom nodes to be accessible during editor time, simply annotate them all using the Tool attribute; that's the intended behavior.

The "test" you're proposing will also result in a Node3D in GDScript, unless the "`@tool`" annotation is used by that custom node.

If you want to limit the scope of what classes are available to tool scripts, that would be an entirely different issue than what you originally proposed... Your original issue seems to only rely on a missunderstanding of how godot is meant to work, rather than a bug.

author: AThousandShips

body:
The documentation you're looking for can be found in the documentation page linked by @raulsntos. Specifically these subsections:
- [Important information](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/plugins/running_code_in_the_editor.html#important-information)
- The note for [editing variables](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/plugins/running_code_in_the_editor.html#editing-variables) specifically says: `Code from other nodes doesn't run in the editor.` This includes the method `GetType`, among others. It *could* be more explicit, but this kind of behavior, and warnings about the fragility of `tool` scripts, is reiterated throughout this page, and the page is apparently already long enough for people to not read it, so I doubt being *more* explicit would help.

(These are just two mentions of these kinds of warnings I've found at a quick glance)

author: KoBeWi

body:
Seems like it's macOS bug (and likely new one, I do not remember anything like this in older versions), auto resize is triggered for windows resized from code (but not manually resized) by the move OS move event handler.

```
....
0x000000019c8ef120 -[NSWindow _setFrameCommon:display:fromServer:] + 2708
0x000000019d35ece8 -[NSWindow(NSScreenLayout) _setFrame:fromAdjustmentToScreen:anchorIfNeeded:animate:] + 1572
0x000000019d35e6b8 -[NSWindow(NSScreenLayout) _setFrame:fromAdjustmentToScreen:anchor:animate:] + 32
0x000000019c9d8174 -[NSWindow _setFrameAfterMove:] + 1088
0x000000019d367ab4 -[NSWindow _endWindowMoveWithEvent:] + 60
....
```

We probably can override `_setFrame:fromAdjustmentToScreen` private method in our `NSWindow` subclass to prevent it.
author: bruvzg

body:
> We probably can override `_setFrame:fromAdjustmentToScreen` private method in our `NSWindow` subclass to prevent it.

Yes, however, this might also disable adjustments we do want in case of moving between monitors with different resolution scaling (which I guess is the intention of this), or (maybe?) resizing down windows that are too large to fit on the new monitor. If it is indeed a macOS bug (I've seen it in Godot at least since macOS 14.5) I guess we could also report it to them and hope it'll be fixed as it's kind of specific and not likely to happen that often in Godot. I've done some searching but haven't found any other mentions of it though. But since other window resizing issues related to multiple-monitor setups are so common it's a hard issue to search for. 

The main use case where you'd encounter this (and how I discovered it) is I guess restoring a window size and position at startup based on saved info from last session. And if you then first move the window and then resize it on the secondary monitor you run into this issue. But if you resize is first and move it second you can avoid it, so there's sort of a workaround for it if you know about it.
author: lostminds

body:
> however, this might also disable adjustments we do want in case of moving between monitors with different resolution scaling (which I guess is the intention of this),

Seems to have no effect on moving between different DPI screens.

>  or (maybe?) resizing down windows that are too large to fit on the new monitor.

It is disabling this, but I'm not sure if OS fitting window to screen is desired behavior (it's adjusting only the size and only when moving manually, so mostly useless).

> I've done some searching but haven't found any other mentions of it though

The only related thing I have found is this https://github.com/iPlug2/iPlug2/commit/dfce8c6344b32fa8789926594e46a63ce31ef99b (overriding `_setFrame` for exactly same reason).
author: bruvzg

body:
> It is disabling this, but I'm not sure if OS fitting window to screen is desired behavior (it's adjusting only the size and only when moving manually, so mostly useless).

I think it's supposed to ensure that parts of the window aren't stuck outside the monitor so you can't reach the edges and resize it down. Which could be a nice service to the user, but perhaps less valuable than avoiding this weird resize issue considering you can resize the windows from the other edges and move it.

For cases like this perhaps a `Window.screen_changed` signal could be a useful addition to allow users to get a notification when the window has moved to a new monitor so they have a chance then to deal with resizing/scaling issues like this themselves.

body: This feature is not supported for samples. We could support eventually base effects for the Web platform, but not custom GDScript ones.

body: So why is it default now?
author: kyanbasu

body:
> So why is it default now?

Please read [my blog post](https://godotengine.org/article/progress-report-web-export-in-4-3/) on the subject.

**TL;DR:** If you're using threads, feel free to revert to streams. But if you're targeting single-threaded exports, streams have huge issues/are unusable, hence the creation of samples. And there's the fact that single-threaded games are the standard on the web, for compatibility and usability reasons.

author: AThousandShips

body:
Explaining this bug in more detail:

Initially, I noticed a z-index bug in Parallax 2D, where the children of each Parallax were out of order—some appeared behind others, even though the hierarchy was correct. Additionally, some items were changing their order even though they belonged to the same node. I had to reset the z-index to the default value of 0 for the behavior to be as expected. While the z-index issue in Parallax2D was resolved by resetting the z-index to 0, I discovered that this bug also affects other 2D nodes. In my scene, for example, some items disappeared—one instance of a moving platform was visible, while another with the same configuration was hidden, even though all child nodes had z-index = 0. I performed several exhaustive tests on the node hierarchy, and the hierarchy is correct. The z-index bug is evident in this new version, 4.3.stable.
author: felipefacundes

body:
Please break it down into clear instructions and add an MRP to make testing this easier:
- A small Godot project which reproduces the issue, with no unnecessary files included. Be sure to not include the `.godot` folder in the archive (but keep `project.godot`).
- Having an MRP is very important for contributors to be able to reproduce the bug in the same way that you are experiencing it. When testing a potential fix for the issue, contributors will use the MRP to validate that the fix is working as intended.
- Drag and drop a ZIP archive to upload it (max 10 MB). **Do not select another field until the project is done uploading.**
- **Note for C# users:** If your issue is *not* C#-specific, please upload a minimal reproduction project written in GDScript. This will make it easier for contributors to reproduce the issue locally as not everyone has a .NET setup available.

body:
`env["CXX"]` reads from the SCons `Environment` object, not from the OS environment. Godot's buildsystem doesn't read OS environment variables, by design to ensure builds are reproducible and configurations intentional. You should pass those values as argument to the `scons` command:
```
scons CC="..." CXX="..."
```
author: akien-mga

body:
@akien-mga This is what I'm passing
```
scons p=linuxbsd target=editor arch=${TARGET_ARCH_NAME} \
    use_llvm=yes \
    use_static_cpp=yes \
    optimize=speed \
    lto=thin \
    progress=yes \
    no_editor_splash=yes \
    num_jobs=${BB_NUMBER_THREADS} \
    ${PACKAGECONFIG_CONFARGS} \
    CC="${CC}" cflags="${CFLAGS}" \
    CXX="${CXX}" cxxflags="${CXXFLAGS}" \
    AS="${AS}" AR="${AR}" RANLIB="${RANLIB}" \
    LINK="${CXX} ${LDFLAGS} -fuse-ld=lld" \
    import_env_vars=PATH,PKG_CONFIG_DIR,PKG_CONFIG_DISABLE_UNINSTALLED,PKG_CONFIG_LIBDIR,PKG_CONFIG_PATH,PKG_CONFIG_SYSROOT_DIR,PKG_CONFIG_SYSTEM_INCLU>
```

body: You're compiling with `use_llvm=yes` but using GCC, which is not supported. `use_llvm` enables logic specific to LLVM/Clang, like the use of `-flo=thin`, which fails with GCC.

Thanks!

What headset and web browser are you using?

WebXR actually doesn't use the action map, that's an OpenXR thing. WebXR itself returns data for either "thumbstick" or "trackpad", and XR Tools has some internal settings that chooses which of those will be mapped to "primary" or "secondary".

In any case, I just tested on a Meta Quest Pro in the Meta Browser (with v69 - there's a bug presently in v68 that affects Godot) and the "thumbstick" was returning correct output.

So, I suspect the problem is either related to the headset or browser, or perhaps there is an XR Tools issue?

body:
I was experiencing this issue through Bluetooth only and it seems to be a Windows Bluetooth bug. When you hold the power button to turn off the PS5 controller, Windows is still showing the controller connected for some reason which causes Godot to never realize it's been disconnected. author: KoBeWi

body:
Use call_deferred() or await the physics tick instead of using a _ready() function.

A _ready() is "this node has joined the SceneTree successfully" and not "every single part of the setup of this node was done".

Plenty of nodes involve server setups with sync queues, those are never "fully ready" in a _ready() function call.

A CSGShape needs to be build first and then synced with the server. In the majority of cases CSG shapes involve multiple nodes under a CSG combination so CSG nodes need to wait for all other SceneTree nodes that may take all part in the same CSG operation. Even if the CSG node would not need to wait for other nodes its freshly build collision shape would still be in the physics sync queue and only available after the next physics sync.
author: smix8

body:
Thanks for the explanation.

I think it would be nice to have this bit mentioned in `RayCast3D` documentation because it can definitely be somewhat confusing for the less experienced users.
author: ItzCog

body:
Because RayCast3D is a physics using node.

It updates its shape and instance transform with the physics tick that is not necessarily in sync with your frame rate so any visuals get just "normal" physics jitter.

Your animation is changing just the node position every frame while the shape position gets updated on every physics tick.
author: smix8

body:
I understand, but the issue is I only started seeing this with 4.3. If you open the test project that I provided in 4.2 the effect is not there. Also in the project that I provided, the maximum FPS is set to 60 which is the same as the physics tick rate and despite this, the ray still lags behind it's actual position significantly, but only in version 4.3.

I should also add that you can clearly see that when the ray is jittering or lagging the ray goes between visually colliding and not colliding with the body, yet the actual collision, that being whether the body is displaying red or not, is not changing, meaning the visual is not matching the actual state of the ray.

author: matheusmdx

body:
The only difference is that before, because a Node3D pushed all its transforms to all the children, it would show a false debug state, more so now with interpolation. The actual shape would move smooth as it would be updated twice, but it was also wrong half the time. The real physics shape was never in that position when it was not updated with the physics sync, it was a visual lie.

With the linked PRs the shape is only update when it is really updated and that reveals to users how physics actually does the update, it jitters just like physics always does because it is not necessarily in sync with the frame rate.

Going back to the old "smooth visuals" does not solve the issue because then you get the "why are my shapes not working, the visuals show they should collide --- yeah because the visuals are lying just to look nice" which is even more dog. If you move your RayCast3D node with the physics step your visuals are correct, everything else is just made up and you would need to force update the node to have the starting position correct. You still can not use an "idle" processing AnimationPlayer or process() function or anything else that is not in sync with physics to move the node.

What could be done is that the debug gets updated when users call the `force_raycast_update()` function in scripts as there is no code path that makes the debug react to anything there.

body:
Yes, the bug still exists in 4.x.

The fix is in PR #96063, but it needs review (might need a rebase by now, but the principal is the same).
It can be difficult to get reviewers who are familiar with the area.

The same fix has been merged in 3.x for 2 years now.

Strictly speaking, the calling code is asking `unproject_position()` to do a calculation that yields an infinite result (via a divide by zero), so you could argue to fix it in the calling code.

But on balance it's more robust to just make the `unproject_position()` robust for this situation, return failure, and return something reasonable that is unlikely to break the calling code. Users, for instance will likely call `unproject_position()` from script and probably don't want to deal with the minutia of this particular pathological situation.

To address this issue, instead of setting the time_scale to 0, I adjusted it to a very small value close to 0 (e.g., 0.00000000000001). This effectively pauses the engine without causing potential complications that might arise from a time_scale of exactly 0.

`Engine.time_scale = 0.00000000000001;`

hope this help others until this bug removed from engine.

body:
Likely a consequence of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/65295 since JSON files are now imported as Resources, and by default every Resource is included in exports. You should be able to exclude these JSON files using the exclude filters:

![](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/be12909d-d8d4-44c2-8a70-269fe21f1e63)

Alternatively, you could try adding an empty [`.gdignore`](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/best_practices/project_organization.html#ignoring-specific-folders) file to the `export` folder. This will prevent Godot from importing any of the Resources in that directory.

body:
That's right, I wasn't even aware of a native Godot ARM64 build until recently, and had assumed it to be an emulation error until now. 

DPI awareness issues sound reasonable, the only reason that I'm leaning more towards platform issues is because I would assume Godot to be used in many high-DPI environments on other hardware platforms without issue because of the platforms that the engine under the hood does support natively. This could instead be a Godot Windows issue more generally, but then I would also expect Windows users of Godot with HighDPI screens to be common enough as well. There is at least one HighDPI Windows open issue in the issue tracker at the moment, and I would personally be surprised if I was the first to have stumbled on a more generic issue, but that's just my hunch so far.

It seems like the common thread across the windows with issues are ones that minimize in what I can only relate as a "Windows 3.11-style". They don't minimize to the taskbar, they minimize to become a tiny titlebar and go to the bottom left of the taskbar.

body:
> Some online forum post recommended enabling MSDF to improve 3D font rendering when the label is further away from the camera. So should I simply disable MSDF again?

MSDF is better for 3D, but it's picky about the fonts. The options are either disable it, use a different font, or edit the outlines in the font to remove intersections.

body:
Fixable by setting RPCNDR version for the platform code (currently done only for `d3d12` module). Not sure if this is caused by MinGW headers, DX headers or `DisplayServer` change, maybe some header is included where it should not be.

```diff
-env.AppendUnique(CPPDEFINES=["D3D12_ENABLED", "RD_ENABLED"])
+env.AppendUnique(CPPDEFINES=["D3D12_ENABLED", "RD_ENABLED", ("__REQUIRED_RPCNDR_H_VERSION__", 475)])
```
author: bruvzg

body:
It seems `__REQUIRED_RPCNDR_H_VERSION__` does get defined by `drivers\d3d12\SCsub`, but #91172 added the include in `display_server.cpp` which is outside of `drivers\d3d12`.

I believe it should be possible to refactor the code so that `display_server.cpp` doesn't need to include RD headers, but I guess defining `__REQUIRED_RPCNDR_H_VERSION__` at a wider scope can fix the issue. (Though I hate that it would force a lot of objects to be recompiled.)
author: alvinhochun

body:
> Though I hate that it would force a lot of objects to be recompiled.

It can be defined directly in the `rendering_device_driver_d3d12.h` header (before any includes and in `if !defined(_MSC_VER)` block) and should cover both driver and display server, but not the rest of code.
author: bruvzg

body:
I believe this is because the compatibility renderer uses sRGB but the forward and mobile renderers use HDR, this is documented but not as clearly as it should be, see [here](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/contributing/development/core_and_modules/internal_rendering_architecture.html#compatibility), it's mentioned [here](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/3d/3d_rendering_limitations.html#color-banding) as well but not clearly

See also:
* https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/88214

body: * I don't think it's a bug. An invalid GDScript is still a resource. If you want to check if a GDScript resource is valid, you can use [`can_instantiate()`](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_script.html#class-script-method-can-instantiate). See also #64065.
author: dalexeev

body:
I believe it would be more intuitive if, instead of the current error message stating that the `new()` method doesn't exist, the error could explicitly indicate that the script cannot be instantiated due to errors in the script. This would make it clearer that the issue lies with the script's validity rather than suggesting that the `new()` method is missing from a seemingly valid `GDScript` object.

Such an error message would better guide users toward understanding the root cause of the issue and encourage the use of `can_instantiate()` to validate scripts before attempting to instantiate them.

Thank you for your input and the reference to the `can_instantiate()` method.

author: detomon

body:
@detomon Good catch, this does fix the problem for SVG icons! Does lead me to a few additional questions though:

- Should this be checked/true by default? I can't think of any reason why someone would want to use an SVG in the Editor but _not_ scale it to match.
- Should **only** SVG assets be allowed as custom `@icon`s? Looks like all the [built-in icons](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/tree/e63c40e59c6650bdba02c2ceff3390ee9273ac46/editor/icons) are SVG and I don't see that `Scale With Editor Scale` option for importing PNG assets, which leads me to believe the Editor prefers SVG for `@icon`. And I wouldn't be against the idea of requiring SVG for custom icons, for the sake of simplicity and ensuring accurate rendering.

The [documentation](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_@gdscript.html#class-gdscript-annotation-icon) doesn't specify a filetype, so at the very least it feels like there's an opportunity for providing clearer user guidance.

author: AThousandShips

body:
## Synopsis
It appears exporting with `NativeAOT` in release mode **breaks** C# projects in v4.3.

## Breakdown
Further testing has revealed some important clues. Here's what I've tried since my last post:

1. I now pool all references that inherit from `Resource`. This is because there may be a bug where custom C# resources are being disposed prematurely as demonstrated here: #83762
2. I thought this may be the reason why there are a significant number of null references that prevent my top level `Node3D` scene from instancing.
3. Although it may have fixed one issue, there seems to be another related one because I get a different error now:

```
USER ERROR: Scene instance is missing.
 at: instantiate (scene/resources/packed_scene.cpp:244)
```

This is the same method that fails above in `packed_scene.cpp` which is `instantiate`; however, the error gets further down.

A part of my testing was using `NativeAOT` on a different platform (in this case Windows) because that is the primary difference between publishing to App Store Connect, and say, Android) and this error did not occur initially. However, when I switched `Export with debug` off, the same exact error that occurs on iOS now happens on Windows. This may be a regression as I have tested `NativeAOT` on Windows with v4.2.2 and this error does not occur.

So it seems that when `NativeAOT` is used in combination with the release option, some Godot methods stop working when accessed from C#. My research shows this may be similar to: #94642

These are two commits that could have introduced the regression 465742d & be4cbee
author: atlasapplications

body:
### More testing has revealed a few important things.

Apparently, when I tested `NativeAOT` in the past, I didn't test it in release mode because I tested every single dev build for 4.3 to see which build introduced the regression going all the way back to the stable 4.2.2 and  every single one of them fails to export with `NativeAOT` on and in release mode. However, when debug is on, the build works correctly. With `NativeAOT` off, debug and release modes work.

However, I don't think this changes that there may be a different error introduced in 4.3. This is because when I launch my project in 4.2.2 this is the error:

```
ERROR: Condition "!sdata.is_valid()" is true. Returning: nullptr
 at: instantiate (scene/resources/packed_scene.cpp:220)
```
This is obviously different than the two other errors above and it's still the same method that's failing which is when I load the packed scene for my top level `Node3D` and then call `instantiate`.

Here's a quick breakdown on how the errors change:
- 4.2.2: the `!sdata.is_valid()` error
- 4.3.dev1: the `Index nprops` error
- 4.3.dev2: oddly it reverts back to `!sdata.is_valid()` error
-  4.3.dev3: back to `Index nprops` error
- 4.3.dev4: `Scene instance is missing` error

author: TCROC

body:
@raulsntos @Calinou Roping you guys in. I can confirm this bug is due to dotnet optimizations.  Changing this line here:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/34f005d810e8967ee0d57afb78f8e0a64f473094/modules/mono/editor/Godot.NET.Sdk/Godot.NET.Sdk/Sdk/Sdk.props#L55C5-L55C64

To be:

```xml
<Optimize Condition=" '$(GodotTargetPlatform)' != 'ios' and '$(Optimize)' == '' ">false</Optimize>
```

Fixes it in my project.  Which leads me to believe this is likely caused by the dotnet compiler "optimizing" things it shouldn't be.  It may even be an ios specific bug on the dotnet side.  So I guess we have a few potential solutions:

1. Disable dotnet optimizations all together for ios.  I personally don't like this option, but its definitely an improvement from random crashes.
1. Add this attribute to each godot interfacing function: https://stackoverflow.com/a/38633044. ``[MethodImpl(MethodImplOptions.NoOptimization | MethodImplOptions.NoInlining)]``.  This of course is assuming that the error is due to a function optimization.  But if it is, this could would be reasonable I think.  Ideally you don't want interfacing / interop / marshalling functions being optimized anyways.
1. Move all classes that we don't want optimized to a separate ``.csproj`` that always has ``<Optimize>false</Optimize>``.  Similar solution to 2, but less verbose.

I'm going to go ahead with option 1 in my fork for now since all of our high performance code is written in Rust.  Our dotnet code is mostly used for UI interfaces and leverage some http sdks.  I'll put in a PR with option 1 as well in case anyone wants a quick solution to the problem so their ios builds stop crashing.
author: TCROC

body:
Thanks for looking into this issue. As you rightly pointed out, disabling optimizations all together, especially for Release builds, isn't our desired long-term direction due to the significant impact on performance and application size.

For now, as a temporary measure, you should be able to explicitly override the `<Optimize>` property to `false` in your own application's `.csproj` file if that unblocks you. However, our goal is to understand the root cause of this crash and find a proper fix that allows optimizations to remain enabled.

Keep in mind that NativeAOT is currently not well supported in Godot, since we don't fully support trimming yet. On iOS, we try to root the GodotSharp and user project assemblies to prevent the trimmer from removing too much, but it's a hack and doesn't guarantee things will work. To ensure NativeAOT works properly we need to fix the warnings reported by .NET when publishing with NativeAOT.

To help us investigate further and work towards that proper fix, could you provide more details about the crash? The errors mentioned earlier in this issue point to a problem potentially occurring in the C++ code related to instantiating Packed Scenes. Specifically, a call stack from the device where the crash occurs would be incredibly helpful in understanding what part of the C# code is leading to that C++ error.

author: Kingwad

author: akien-mga

body:
Thank you for the report!

In the past it was reported that it was impossible to write proper window rules without different ids[^1], so I think that this is the right solution.

I eventually came to the conclusion that multiple desktop entries are the right choice, which seems quite consistent to me as they're conceptually different applications, even if they come from the same executable.

> The initially apparent workaround of having separate .desktop files for each context would introduce the bug of having Godot show up multiple times in the Application menu.

I do agree that it would be quite unpleasant to see. The documentation talks about the `NoDisplay`[^2] tag which seems perfect for the job, as it apparently hides the entry from the menu while still letting it get parsed (e.g. for media type associations). I think we can use this tag to write different entries but I didn't try it yet.

Note that I have very little experience with this stuff. As we say it here, TIWAGOS (take it with a grain of salt) :D

I hope this sheds some light on the current situation regarding app ids and packaging.

[^1]: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/57025#issuecomment-1412415700

[^2]: https://specifications.freedesktop.org/desktop-entry-spec/latest/recognized-keys.html
author: deralmas

body:
I never really understood the point and the validity of the "USER" prefix. It seems to be added based on somewhat weird conditions which I'm not sure are what was intended.

As you can see in the code, it adds `USER` for errors which are not `p_editor_notify`, i.e. not meant for the toaster I guess? Seems very weird to me and in my experience, not related to whether the error originated in user code or editor code.

CC @groud who implemented this back then IIRC.
author: akien-mga

body:
> As you can see in the code, it adds USER for errors which are not p_editor_notify, i.e. not meant for the toaster I guess? Seems very weird to me and in my experience, not related to whether the error originated in user code or editor code.

Yeah, `p_editor_notify` is mainly used to choose whether or not the error shows up in the editor as a toast. I think the rationale here was that errors with `p_editor_notify` would be user-caused errors and those without it would be internal. But in that case I think the output are inverse.

Anyway, I think it can be removed, it's probably useless and confusing.

> CC @groud who implemented this back then IIRC.

I was about to say it was not me, then checked with git blame. Turns out it was me. Typical:
![Two-Spiderman-Meme-Template-on-Pointing-at-Each-Other-263755328](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9594ea48-ac3a-4ce8-a7f5-7319ddac01da)
author: groud

body:
Yeah I agree that we can remove it. IIRC I had a quick look a while ago, and basically removing it means the whole `p_editor_notify` bool could be removed in all the multiple level of logging features we have, as it was only used for this (needs confirming).

Though depending on what's exposed, some care might be needed to not break compat.
author: akien-mga

body:
> Yeah I agree that we can remove it. IIRC I had a quick look a while ago, and basically removing it means the whole p_editor_notify bool could be removed in all the multiple level of logging features we have, as it was only used for this (needs confirming).

Hmm, I meant to just remove the`USER` prefix. The bool is needed for the editor toast. I don't know if there would be an other solution for it though, we kind of need a way to notify a logger an error needs to be popped without introducing editor code in the game code. That's why I added the boolean.
author: groud

body:
> > Yeah I agree that we can remove it. IIRC I had a quick look a while ago, and basically removing it means the whole p_editor_notify bool could be removed in all the multiple level of logging features we have, as it was only used for this (needs confirming).
> 
> Hmm, I meant to just remove the`USER` prefix. The bool is needed for the editor toast. I don't know if there would be an other solution for it though, we kind of need a way to notify a logger an error needs to be popped without introducing editor code in the game code. That's why I added the boolean.

Right indeed, out of the 35(!) files where this bool is passed along, it's used meaningfully in
```
editor/gui/editor_toaster.cpp
168:    if (p_editor_notify || (show_all_setting == 0 && in_dev) || show_all_setting == 1) {
172:            if (!p_editor_notify) {
```
and so it shouldn't be removed.
author: akien-mga

body:
Hello, I'm looking to start contributing to Godot to give back since I've been using it for a few smaller projects and am beginning my first major project with the engine. I saw this issue was marked as 'good first issue' and would like to give it a go to get used to the flow of contributing to Godot. (I have experience making PRs at work and to another open source project, and some experience with C++, but I am unfamiliar with SCons or the Godot internals.)

My understanding from the discussion so far is that either:

1. lines 87-91 in `core/io/logger.cpp` can be simplified to just the function call on line 88
2. line 90 in `core/io/logger.cpp` can be updated to use a clearer prefix than 'USER', like 'CUSTOM' (or potentially 'DEVELOPER' or 'MANUAL'?)

Is one approach or the other preferable? An alternative option could potentially be to have the internal errors use an 'ENGINE' or 'INTERNAL' prefix, and leave developer emitted errors as just 'ERROR'/'WARNING'. I think that this approach could be clearer for a new or inexperienced user, rather than wondering where some `.cpp` file is in their project, but could be undesirable for other reasons.
author: CreatedBySeb

body:
> lines 87-91 in `core/io/logger.cpp` can be simplified to just the function call on line 88

This approach is the consensus so far. There's a few other places that need to be adjusted similarly, which add a "(User)" suffix to some in-editor messages:
```
platform/windows/os_windows.cpp
175:            err_str += " (User)\n";

editor/editor_log.cpp
56:             err_str += " (User)";
```

body:
I have drafted a PR #96168 and validated it works as expected for the case outlined in the original issue on macos.arm64. I do not have access to Windows so cannot test it on that platform.

In the case of the `platform/windows/os_windows.cpp` modification I opted to move the end line to the initial assignments rather than have the second assignment given it no longer seemed necessary.

In the three functions modified, the `p_editor_notify` parameter seems to no longer used, is it preferable to remove it from these functions and then update their usages or leave in the redundant parameter? I am unsure if this would result in compatibility breakage as I don't know if the functions here are exposed.
author: CreatedBySeb

body:
> In the three functions modified, the `p_editor_notify` parameter seems to no longer used, is it preferable to remove it from these functions and then update their usages or leave in the redundant parameter? I am unsure if this would result in compatibility breakage as I don't know if the functions here are exposed.

AFAIK it cannot be removed, these are virtual functions so they all need to have the same prototype, and the arguments is used in one of the more specific implementations (in `editor/gui/editor_toaster.cpp`).

body:
Godot 4.3 has more features, which means more code, and thus an increase in binary size.

14 MB increase is significant though and should be looked into. Part of it is probably the new D3D12 renderer, but I wouldn't expect it to be that heavy.

*Edit:* Checked some export templates I had installed locally:
```
67M 4.2.2.stable/windows_release_x86_64.exe
67M 4.3.dev1/windows_release_x86_64.exe
67M 4.3.dev2/windows_release_x86_64.exe
75M 4.3.dev3/windows_release_x86_64.exe
76M 4.3.dev4/windows_release_x86_64.exe
77M 4.3.beta1/windows_release_x86_64.exe
80M 4.3.beta3/windows_release_x86_64.exe
81M 4.3.rc1/windows_release_x86_64.exe
81M 4.3.stable/windows_release_x86_64.exe
```

So it went from 67M to 75M in dev3, which is indeed the first release where official builds include D3D12 support (after https://github.com/godotengine/godot-build-scripts/pull/92).

There's another minor 3M bump between beta1 and beta3 worth looking into too.

CC @bruvzg

body: I'm wondering if the implementation in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/83087 would be better, given the issues with `get_gravity`. @mihe thoughts?
author: aaronfranke

body:
This specific problem wouldn't be fixed by something like #83087. As hinted at by @AThousandShips already, the area overlaps are evaluated as part of stepping the physics simulation, which you'd need to have up-to-date no matter which approach you take.

I'm not sure if this is really documented anywhere, but this limitation applies to a number of physics-related things in Godot, like the contact reporting and the various signals emitted (e.g. `body_entered`). I wouldn't really consider this a bug.

The only way I can see this being done (short of custom stepping + rollback, which isn't a thing in Godot, and seems overkill here) would be to use [`intersect_shape`](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_physicsdirectspacestate2d.html#class-physicsdirectspacestate2d-method-intersect-shape) with `collide_with_areas` set to `true`, to figure out what areas you'd be overlapping with at that point and then calculate the total gravity yourself based on their `priority` and `gravity_space_override`, but that is not trivial.

Also (and this is borderline off-topic) there is another bug lurking in the current implementation of `get_gravity`, which I touched upon briefly [here](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/84640#issuecomment-1927117203), and which would be fixed by something like #83087, where if your `CharacterBody3D` (or some other kinematic body) is just standing perfectly still, and you have some `Area3D` with `gravity_space_override` moving around and entering/exiting the body, then the gravity reported by `get_gravity` will become stale, as gravity is only computed for active/awake bodies, and kinematic bodies are only considered active/awake during the single tick after which they moved.

I need to make a separate issue for that.

body: @Calinou after doing some more testing, it actually has to do with using the Viewport stretch mode, so not a bug. The projects I tested in were low-res, and scaled up nodes filter fine, so I guess there wasn't enough pixels to filter properly? Setting the resolution to match my monitor gave correct results, but nothing lower than that works. I don't know that that's an issue though. Either way, I added the MRP to be safe.

body:
> Can't reproduce it. Since it seems to fail to load some font, it might be related to an issue with a system fallback font.

I remembered something; the night before this error occurred, I adjusted the theme in the editor settings. On the Mac, it doesn’t switch to the dark theme correctly automatically, so I turned off the option to follow the system theme and then toggled between different editor themes, ultimately choosing the Default theme. I didn’t open Godot again after that until the next day when I started Godot and the project was very slow to load, followed by a large number of these errors. I suspect that changing the theme might have caused this issue to happen.

body:
After a deep research, I think the problem is not with the engine, but probably with some system fonts from macOS 15, /System/Library/PrivateFrameworks/FontServices.framework/Versions/A/Resources/Reserved/PingFangUI.ttc for example. The font lacks the 'loca' table, and this makes it unable to be loaded with freetype library.

author: CVSJason

body: Fine. There's still no loca table in PingFangUI.ttc in the latest macOS beta. Maybe Godot somehow managed to skip these broken font files. I wonder if this is due to a change in the system.
author: CVSJason

body:
Related upstream FreeType [issue](https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/freetype/freetype/-/issues/1281), seems like new `PingFangUI` is using undocumented `hvgl` table instead of `loca`, so they changed font to a new proprietary format, and it's unlikely it will be fixed in new OS updates.

> Maybe Godot somehow managed to skip these broken font files

Done in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/97693
author: bruvzg

body: I think we can close this then as worked around by #97693, and the actual issue with the `PingFangUI` font needs to be handled by FreeType or Apple.

body:
Tested on Fedora 40 with the system provided mbedtls 2.28.8 and it seems to work fine.
I then compiled the (currently only being tested in rawhide) 3.6.1 version, and it still works fine.

So I finally tested building 3.6.0, and this one does exhibit the issue. So it's an upstream mbedtls bug which has been fixed already. Arch Linux should be able to fix the issue by updating their mbedtls package from 3.6.0 to 3.6.1 (which also fixes security issues so it's something they ought to do sooner than later anyway).

I don't have an Arch Linux SSO account, but if one of you does, it would be good to mention that on https://gitlab.archlinux.org/archlinux/packaging/packages/godot/-/issues/3 so that the godot and mbedtls maintainers there are aware.

author: AThousandShips

body:
This is not a bug, you are adding *two new* points to a gradient that already has two points, the default constructed gradient has two points, `[ {0, Color.BLACK}, {1, Color.WHITE} ]`

You need to instead do:
```gdscript
gradient_by_script.set_color(0, Color.RED)
gradient_by_script.set_color(1, Color.GREEN)
```
Or, before you add the points, do:
```gdscript
gradient_by_script.remove_point(0)
gradient_by_script.remove_point(0)
```

I don't know what would need to be documented further, but this isn't a bug but user error
author: AThousandShips

body:
> Please test with a recent version like 4.2.2 or 4.3, you're on a pre-release for 4.2

Ops, my bad; that slipped out of my mind.

> This is not a bug, you are adding _two new_ points to a gradient that already has two points, the default constructed gradient has two points, `[ {0, Color.BLACK}, {1, Color.WHITE} ]`

Oh, right, now that all makes sense. Should I change the title of this issue?

---
> I don't know what would need to be documented further, but this isn't a bug but user error

At the moment, the only hint I could have that the constructor added 2 points in the documentation is the line
> [PackedColorArray](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_packedcolorarray.html#class-packedcolorarray) colors = PackedColorArray(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1)

Which I think is not very legible. I could also have figured that out based on how a new Gradient resource is created in the editor, but still, I don't think things are ideal in this regard. What about adding something in the documentation of the Gradient::add_point() ? Something like:
> **Note**: The Gradient constructor adds two points, [ {0, Color.BLACK}, {1, Color.WHITE} ]
author: neoentest2

body:
> Note: The Gradient constructor adds two points, [ {0, Color.BLACK}, {1, Color.WHITE} ]

That isn't true though, it doesn't as such, it's just the default value

But I think the default value of a property should be the place to show what is going on, that is what it's for
author: AThousandShips

body:
> That isn't true though, it doesn't as such, it's just the default value

Got it, just saying that I think it should be stated more clearly in the Gradient documentation. I know that default values are shown, but

> [ {0, Color.BLACK}, {1, Color.WHITE} ]

is way more clear then

> [PackedColorArray]colors = PackedColorArray(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1)

It probably won't fool me again, but it gave me some troubles and I would like others not to experience them too.
author: neoentest2

body:
There are many places where default values aren't mentioned in the description, we *could* add a less specific description of just "initialized as a linear gradient from black to white" or something 

I'm not sure people who miss this or don't check if there's any points already will be helped by the documentation spelling it out more directly
author: AThousandShips

body: Understanding your point of view, and since there are a ton of important and open issues, I think I'd better close this one.
author: neoentest2

body:
after putting some debug into CallQueue::push_callablep() i found out that before the Object was deleted while awaiting a callback. flood, there is a flood of push_callablep of PathFollow3D::set_position (and then the error message about the queue being full). 

The game uses  several pathfollows that get updated every _physics_process, perhaps is that which is too heavy, piles up and freezes the engine, as i see in its implementation update_transform is deferred so i guess is not guaranteed to be finished before the next _physics_process()

Also disabling all the updates of the PathFollow3Ds seems to make the problem not happen

author: matheusmdx

body:
It looks like the problem is a null pointer being passed to `Node3DEditorViewport::_is_node_locked` as a result of the changed code in `Node3DEditorViewport::_select_clicked` in the case that there is no editable parent of the clicked Node3D:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/142d33211cb4276dee6f797117981af983471b9e/editor/plugins/node_3d_editor_plugin.cpp#L741-L773

From debugging the provided MRP, it appears that the new code on lines 753-760 that walks up the scene tree to find the nearest selectable parent sets `selected` to null in the case that there is no selectable parent (such as in the case with the tool script). This null pointer is then passed into `_is_node_locked` on line 773, which does not check if the passed pointer is null and attempts to call `get_meta` on the null pointer, causing the bad memory access and crash.

To avoid a crash, there should probably be a null pointer check on the `selected` pointer, either inside of `_is_node_locked` (not sure what the correct default would be), or before attempting to check if the node is locked in `_select_clicked`, replacing line 773 with: 

```c++
if (p_allow_locked || (selected != nullptr && !_is_node_locked(selected))) {
```

similar to the check on line 755. Making this change solves the problem on macos.arm64, with the click having no effect rather than crashing the editor, which I believe is the intended behaviour. If this is the correct approach I can put that small change in a PR.

body: Godot 4.2 forced position integer snapping, that was removed due to popular demand. In Godot 4.3 it allows to use floats and they get up and downscaled. So all the float precision issues with certain layouts can now survive that would have been removed by the old integer snap. You need to snap or otherwise prevent those situations manually now.
author: smix8

body: Thanks for your answer. Could you tell me what settings I should modify to have integer snap ?
author: rob1gat

body:
There is no direct setting but you can e.g. enable the editor toolbar snapping so that your positions are not running with some weird float numbers, that alone can help a lot.

In general you also want to avoid creating long-distances edges that are only very slightly skewed or rounded shapes that have more edges, because those all gives you more often those kind of tiny polygons that may have difficulties to rasterize properly, it is very layout depending.

If nothing helps loop over the vertices in script and snap them manually, that is also what the official navmesh chunk demo does because float ops are never that precise and you dont know how the navmesh baking will partition things upfront.
author: smix8

body:
Thanks but my polygons already have integer snapped verticed, and my outlines too. Do you mean I should edit the baked navmesh ?

I am absolutely not experienced with navigation baking, but could the [ClipperLib::SimplifyPaths()](http://www.angusj.com/clipper2/Docs/Units/Clipper/Functions/SimplifyPaths.htm) function be applied to the produced navmesh (with epsilon = cell_size) in order to get rid of these tiny polygons ?
author: rob1gat

body:
To really fix this in scripts alone you would need to not only snap the vertices of the baked navmesh but also remove any "duplicates", and fix the polygon indices in turn if removed.

The reason why that tiny polygon that has rasterization issues even gets baked is because the general scale is tiny, that white "collision" shape has a lot of tiny small edges and the agent_radius bake size set to 7 is uneven. All those things together make a very suboptimal bake layout so you end up with more issues to rasterize the resulting edges in the navigation map. E.g. just changing the agent_radius to 6 or 8 removed those tiny edges alone.
author: smix8

body:
> To really fix this in scripts alone you would need to not only snap the vertices of the baked navmesh but also remove any "duplicates", and fix the polygon indices in turn if removed.

Are there any obstacles to adding this process to the navigation mesh baking ? Maybe performances ?
author: rob1gat

body: Performance and because it is not a general fix for bad combinations of layouts and settings. As soon as users rotate and scale or combine with other regions or have different settings its the same issue all over again with rasterization issues. The real fix is to find a combination between geometry and settings that works for a project and the baking can not guess this for users. Same reason why you get light leaks when baking light maps if the mix of geometry and settings just does not work well together.
author: smix8

body:
Looking at the code of `NavMeshGenerator2D::generator_bake_from_source_geometry_data()`, it seems that clipper `InflatePaths()` function is called ([line 957](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/modules/navigation/2d/nav_mesh_generator_2d.cpp#L957)) but `path_solution` is not simplified then.

To quote clipper documentation (about `SimplifyPaths()` function): 
> This function is strongly recommended following [offsetting](http://www.angusj.com/clipper2/Docs/Units/Clipper/Functions/InflatePaths.htm) (ie inflating/shrinking), especially when offsetting paths multiple times. Offsetting often creates tiny segments that don't improve path quality.

I (humbly) believe that calling this method could prevent some tiny triangle issues. Moreover, it could improve baking performances since some boolean operations are then performed on `path_solution`.

What do you think about this ?
author: rob1gat

body: I remember I had some commented-out remnant code while developing the 2D navmesh baking using the clipper simplification (RamadaDouglasClass or something back then) and while it worked for some cases it mess-up a lot of others so I removed it again. That was a while ago so if someone else wants to spend time on re-testing this be my guest.

author: mangiavellano

body:
> I've checked all the monitors specs listed in this thread and it seems that all of them are above 144hz, could be a lead?

In this case, the issue is that the editor is capped to 145 FPS by default due to the **Low Processor Mode Sleep Usec** Editor Setting that defaults to `6900`. (The maximum FPS that can be reached is determined by `1000000.0/6900`.)

If you change it to `4100` on 240 Hz and restart the editor, the issue *should* disappear, at least if the CPU/GPU can keep up.

We should revise this editor setting to use a value that matches the fastest's monitor refresh rate by default, so that it can fully make use of high refresh rate monitors out of the box. This probably involves adding an enum editor setting to choose between an automatic value and the custom existing value (and changing this custom value to be expressed in FPS rather than µspf, as it's more user-friendly).

I also have V-Sync disabled in the Editor Settings here; see if that helps too. Enabling Single Window Mode should further help reduce lag (as new windows won't spawn anymore and [compete with V-Sync rates](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/93014#issuecomment-2197799816)), but it will disable some functionality in the editor.

author: hayahane

body:
The problem seems to be in `void editor_register_icons()` in `editor\themes\editor_icons.cpp`. This function is called when the `Theme` is updated, and to regenerate the icons it goes through each entry in `editor_icons_names` which is defined in as a huge list of built in names in `editor\themes\editor_icons.gen.h`

Problem seems to be that custom icons are not included in this list, and the function doesn't seem to have a way to add them, so they don't get their icons regenerated when the function is called. Need to find where the custom icon details are stored.
author: TheSofox

body: This should be done using the `editor/convert_colors_with_editor_theme` import option, which currently only handles color remapping for light theme, but not the saturation modifier.
author: Calinou

body:
> This should be done using the `editor/convert_colors_with_editor_theme` import option, which currently only handles color remapping for light theme, but not the saturation modifier.

Sadly, this option is only respected when importing. When theme related settings are changed, these assets won't be reimported, and I would argue whether we should do so since it may cause a huge amount of time to scan and reimport.

The way current `@icon` annotation works make it hard to unify icon behaviours:
- Not able to use icons in theme.
- Hard to deal with asset importing.

Since 4.5, all editor icons are stored in theme as `SVGTexture`s, maybe we should remove `editor/convert_colors_with_editor_theme` import option. Therefore, we can add a check when loading `SVGTexture` icons and make it sync with editor theme.
author: hayahane

body:
Succeeded to make the icon change with this option like this:
- When this option changes, clear all icon path cache.
- `SceneTreeEditor` have to reload the icon, that's when it checks if it's a `SVGTexture` and set saturation.

| Saturation 2.0 | Saturation 1.0 |

body:
Howdy, thanks for reporting! I'm adding this to the XWayland tracker to let other users know.

Quick note in case you're not aware of: if you all see a different "native" window for each popup you're using the X11 backend through XWayland, as the Wayland backend is single-window-mode-only for now.

The single window option mentioned above is also settable from the editor preferences, without needing to edit the desktop entry. To do so, open the editor settings and tick the checkbox in "Interface -> Editor -> Single Window Mode" (`interface/editor/single_window_mode`)

If you feel adventurous you might also try the editor with the experimental wayland backend by turning on "Run -> Platforms -> Prefer Wayland" (`run/platform/linuxbsd/prefer_wayland`) ;)

Note that both of these changes will apply only to the editor, and not the running game.

author: Corso-1

body:
This is not a bug, it's a simple user error.

@Corso-1, you'd need to `queue_redraw()` once more after the mouse button is released, so the draw commands for this specific CanvasItem will be cleared, and the enqueued `_draw()` call will result in not adding any new commands because of `Allow_Draw == false`. Otherwise the CanvasItem is being redrawn using the last provided set of draw commands (3 rects in the MRP).

Your CanvasItem is being rendered every frame (as Viewport is being updated), it's not like it is drawn only after `queue_redraw()` is called. All `queue_redraw()` does is it clears draw commands for the given CanvasItem, and it triggers drawing routine which includes: `NOTIFICATION_DRAW`, `draw` signal, `_draw` method override (e.g. built-in nodes issue their draw commands on `NOTIFICATION_DRAW`).

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/db76de5de8a415b29be4c7dd84b99bd0fe260822/scene/main/canvas_item.cpp#L132-L151

---

A little simplified (and "fixed") script from the MRP:
```gdscript
extends Control

func _draw() -> void:
	var MousePos: Vector2 = get_local_mouse_position() # <-- `draw_x` methods expect local coordinates

	draw_rect(Rect2(MousePos,Vector2(10,10)),Color(1,1,0,.05),true)
	draw_rect(Rect2(MousePos+Vector2(0,10),Vector2(10,10)),Color(1,1,1,.1),true)
	draw_rect(Rect2(MousePos+Vector2(0,20),Vector2(10,10)),Color(0,1,0,.2),true)

func _process(_delta: float) -> void:
	visible = Input.is_mouse_button_pressed(MOUSE_BUTTON_LEFT)
	if visible:
		queue_redraw()
```
Here I didn't need to `queue_redraw()` once more after a mouse release because I'm hiding the CanvasItem instead keeping it visible but with zero draw commands.

---

Seems like the documentation might need to be improved.
author: kleonc

body:
Hi Kleonc, thank you muchly for the detailed explanation. Being a newb I'm completely lost for a few things. Greatly appreciated.

Though, I've been testing again, but am not sure of the best case for when you 'emit' a signal to activate the draw.

For example: Regarding the skidding tyres. I emit a signal to the control to draw lines.
Basically:
If Grippy_Slide > 5:
 Skidding.emit(body)

From there the Control picks up the car body and figures out tyre position, length of line etc. then asks the _draw() function to draw it in. 

So, the skidding for the driver and all the NPC cars occurs only when above a threshold. (Which currently produces heavy termination points).

What does one do in this kind of case? I've been wracking my brain, but no clean solution is apparent, unless I tell it to update the subviewport each cycle, as in, queue_redraw even if nothing is sent (empty skid). Is that what we have to do?
author: Corso-1

body:
@Corso-1 This is a bugtracker, not a helpdesk. For asking this type of help questions please use other [community channels](https://godotengine.org/community/), e.g. [Godot Forum](https://forum.godotengine.org/). Sorry but we don't want users to ask here _"How do I implement X in my project?"_ etc., such questions do not belong here.

But since you present yourself as "a newb", I got to praise you: this issue report is super clear. Easy to undersand the issue, your setup, your expectations, etc. If you'd ask for help about your project-specific issues on the Godot Forum etc., with a similar amount of details, then I'm quite sure you'll get the help you're looking for. So thank you for a clear report, and good luck with the project! :slightly_smiling_face:

---

Regarding:

> What does one do in this kind of case? I've been wracking my brain, but no clean solution is apparent, unless I tell it to update the subviewport each cycle, as in, queue_redraw even if nothing is sent (empty skid). Is that what we have to do?

Note that you currently do update the subviewport always, you have set `render_target_update_mode = SubViewport.UPDATE_ALWAYS` (via the inspector). So you update even when you do not `queue_redraw()` items within such subviewport (that's why you had this issue in the first place: you didn't clear the draw commands and the subviewport kept updating).

There's of course no need to always-update a non-clearing subviewport. You could instead set it initially to `render_target_update_mode = SubViewport.UPDATE_NEVER`, and anytime you want something to be drawn in such subviewport you'd set `render_target_update_mode = SubViewport.UPDATE_ONCE`, so draw will happen only the next frame (after which it will auto swap to `render_target_update_mode = SubViewport.UPDATE_NEVER`).

How you'll tell the drawing CanvasItem what/how needs to be drawn is totally up to you (whether you'd use some global reference (and direct calls), or signals (e.g. an event bus), or whatever else).

The manuals/tutorials etc. part of the on-line docs are in the https://github.com/godotengine/godot-docs repo. There you have more images. If you have specific suggestions on improving some of the existing docs, adding new sections, etc. you could report it in there.

author: Chubercik

body:
This does seem to be caused by constant folding performed by the analyzer. I believe normally array addition is done at runtime because at compile time, due to folding, it would cause the arrays with the same content to have the same reference.

The reason why this expression is folded is because the identifiers are constant and so they go through this condition when reducing the addition:
 https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9e6098432aac35bae42c9089a29ba2a80320d823/modules/gdscript/gdscript_analyzer.cpp#L3028
We could add a check here to check that the builtin type of the identifier is safe to fold. I would be happy to do this but I think we need a bit more discussion because adding this would break compat since expressions like `const C = A + B` (where A and B are const arrays) would begin to error.

EDIT: I forgot to mention that we already don't treat addition of array literals as constant expressions e.g. `const C = ["a"] + ["b"]` currently errors, this is inconsistent with the current behaviour of `const C = A + B` not erroring.
author: girdenis-p

body:
Thanks for the analysis! I've been investigating this and I believe the compatibility concern can be addressed.

The codebase already has a pattern for this in `GDScriptAnalyzer::reduce_call()` (lines 3260-3283 in `modules/gdscript/gdscript_analyzer.cpp`) which uses a `safe_to_fold flag` for reference types. When an expression isn't marked as `is_constant`, the `make_expression_reduced_value()` function (line 5267) serves as a fallback to compute values for const declarations.

So `const C = A + B` wouldn't errorm, it would work through the fallback mechanism.

Using the existing pattern as reference, this looks to be a pretty self contained fix so I'll work on a PR for this.

No, but I'll try to take a look soon and include it in my PR if the fix turns out to be minor.

The fix for const array concatenation in dynamic context, as you mentioned, breaks the valid case of array concatenation in constant context. The `make_*_reduced_value()` mechanism will avoid this regression, so my PR should be considered a necessary dependency for this fix.
author: dalexeev

body:
I believe this is a feature, not a bug (this behavior can be configured during import).

From my understanding, the tracks that are removed are position tracks which are not targeting the skeleton root.

These are removed because of the setting "Unimportant Positions" (default on) in the Remove Tracks section of the importer when a BoneMap is used.

The idea is that position animations on one model often do not work properly on other models, and hence for the purpose of retargeting, it is often desirable to remove most position tracks.

Let me know if turning off Unimportant Positions solves the issue for you.

I would be interested to know more about your usecase: If you do retargeting, why is it important to have this specific track be animationd. If not for retargeting, what is the usecase for renaming bones with the bonemap?
author: lyuma

body:
@lyuma Good catch! it was indeed the default "Unimportant Positions" checkbox that was leaving some of the tracks behind. I feel a bit embarrassed now for not exploring that option.

I partially forgot the exact thing I was testing here but it was mostly an attempt to reuse as much as a I can from the Mixamo animations you can download at www.mixamo.com (and other animations that I got from some other resources) for my characters that have a more simple skeleton, with less bones. The first step to achieve this would be to use a common set of bone names that all those animations will use in Godot, then test, and manually edit some of the animations as needed.
 
I believe It does actually help to have those position tracks imported so for instance for a jump or a fall animation, I would get a closer result if I keep those position tracks (maybe not perfect) than if they are removed and I have to manually add all the positioning myself.
In any case it allows me to at least manually select which ones to remove if I think it is better to remove them than to try to edit it so they work with my character.

Thanks a lot for resolving this!

author: dalexeev

body:
This is by design, `Unused Variable` is for local variables, as the documentation says

Member variables can be unused as they could be:
* Used by a derived class
* Used externally

It not being used in the same script is not an indication of anything being wrong, unlike a private variable which *should* be expected to be used in the same script, and a local variable which should be used locally

Feature proposals are tracked [here](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals), please open a proposal instead.
